questions.question: The SRE book argues that pursuing 100% availability for most user-facing services is economically irrational because:
options: The marginal cost of each additional nine of reliability grows superlinearly while user-perceived benefit flattens
Cloud providers contractually forbid five-nines targets on shared hardware
Users always prefer faster feature delivery over any measurable reliability
100% availability is technically impossible only for batch pipelines, not for online APIs
questions.explanation: Chapter 3 frames reliability as a risk-management problem: each nine costs disproportionately more in redundancy, testing, and operational overhead, while users rarely notice differences beyond a well-chosen SLO. SRE explicitly embraces a non-zero failure rate as the rational target.

questions.question: A product team proposes raising an API's availability SLO from 99.9% to 99.99% without additional engineering investment. From an SRE risk perspective, the strongest objection is:
options: Tighter SLOs shrink the error budget, reducing permissible launch risk and requiring costly reliability work to remain achievable
Higher SLOs automatically eliminate the need for postmortems after incidents
99.99% is always the minimum acceptable target for any HTTP service
SLO changes require no stakeholder alignment because they are purely internal metrics
questions.explanation: Embracing risk means choosing an SLO that balances user benefit and engineering cost. Tightening the target without investment makes the error budget vanish, forcing either missed SLOs or frozen velocity — undermining the negotiated dev/SRE model described in chapters 3 and 4.

questions.question: A service targets 99.95% monthly availability. Approximately how much downtime budget does that allow per 30-day month?
options: About 22 minutes
About 4.3 minutes
About 2.2 hours
About 43 minutes
questions.explanation: 99.95% permits 0.05% unavailability: 0.0005 × 30 × 24 × 60 ≈ 21.6 minutes per month. SRE candidates should fluently translate nines into error budgets — the quantitative foundation for launch and incident decisions in chapter 4.

questions.question: When defining an availability SLI as 'successful responses / valid requests,' which request class should typically be excluded from the denominator?
options: Requests that fail due to client-side errors such as malformed input the service correctly rejects with 4xx
All requests that exceed the latency SLO threshold
Requests from internal monitoring probes regardless of outcome
Every request including those blocked by upstream DDoS mitigation before reaching the service
questions.explanation: Chapter 4 stresses SLIs that reflect user-perceived reliability. Client-caused 4xx errors are often excluded because they do not indicate service failure. The exact policy must be documented, but counting user mistakes as downtime distorts SLOs and error budgets.

questions.question: For a latency SLI on a search API, which formulation best aligns with SRE practice?
options: Proportion of queries completing within 500 ms at the 95th percentile over a rolling 28-day window
Average latency across all requests in the last hour
Maximum latency observed on any single request during peak traffic
Median latency of requests originating from the engineering office network only
questions.explanation: Chapter 4 recommends distribution-aware SLIs — typically tail percentiles over meaningful windows — because averages hide outliers that degrade user experience. Rolling windows smooth noise while preserving long-term accountability.

questions.question: An error budget policy states that when 75% of the quarterly budget is consumed with six weeks remaining, the team should:
options: Treat this as an early warning: increase reliability focus, review recent changes, and tighten launch criteria before budget exhaustion
Immediately freeze all development until the next quarter regardless of remaining budget
Lower the SLO target mid-quarter to restore budget without engineering changes
Ignore the burn rate because only 100% budget consumption triggers action
questions.explanation: Error budgets are leading indicators, not binary switches. Chapter 4 describes tiered responses as burn rate accelerates — early intervention preserves remaining budget and avoids emergency freezes. Secretly moving SLO targets destroys trust in the model.

questions.question: A composite service depends on three backends each with independent 99.9% SLOs. Without redundancy, what is the approximate theoretical ceiling on end-to-end availability?
options: Roughly 99.7% (product of individual availabilities)
Exactly 99.9% because the slowest dependency sets the floor
100% if the composite layer retries all failures
99.99% because SLOs add when services are chained
questions.explanation: Serial dependency availability multiplies: 0.999³ ≈ 0.997. Chapter 4's SLI/SLO material implies composite services need stricter component targets or redundancy to meet user-facing goals — a common system-design interview insight.

questions.question: Why do SRE teams typically set internal SLOs slightly stricter than external SLAs?
options: To consume the SLA margin as an error budget buffer, reducing contractual breach risk while preserving a measurable internal target
Because legal teams require internal targets to exceed contractual commitments
SLAs are always stricter than SLOs by industry regulation
There is no relationship; SLOs and SLAs must be identical
questions.explanation: Chapter 4 explains the SLO-as-buffer pattern: internal targets tighter than customer-facing SLAs give teams measurable budget to spend on velocity while maintaining contractual headroom. Breaching an SLA triggers external consequences; exhausting an SLO triggers internal policy.

questions.question: Which characteristic is NOT part of the SRE book's definition of toil?
options: Work that permanently improves the system's architecture on first execution
Manual and repetitive execution
Triggered by operational events rather than proactive design
Grows proportionally with service growth if left unaddressed
questions.explanation: Chapter 5 defines toil as manual, repetitive, automatable operational work that scales with service size — not engineering that durably improves the system. One-time architectural improvements are valuable SRE work, not toil.

questions.question: An SRE team tracks toil as 45% of time. Leadership asks why not automate the remaining ticket queue immediately. The principled response is:
options: Automation itself requires engineering capacity; prioritize highest-volume toil with best ROI while preserving time for SLO work, launches, and on-call
45% toil is above the 50% cap so no action is needed until it reaches 50%
All toil should be eliminated in one quarter regardless of other priorities
Toil above 30% means the team should stop participating in design reviews
questions.explanation: Chapter 5 recommends capping toil (commonly ~50%) and investing remaining capacity in automation — but automation is not free. Prioritization by impact, balancing on-call and project work, reflects realistic SRE capacity management.

questions.question: Rotating through the same manual remediation playbook every on-call shift without automating the underlying fix primarily increases:
options: Ongoing toil that scales with incident frequency and prevents engineering investment in durable reliability improvements
Error budget because manual work is excluded from SLO calculations
Team morale sustainably because operators gain deep system familiarity
Launch velocity because on-call experience replaces design review
questions.explanation: Repeated manual remediation is textbook toil: it returns each shift, grows with traffic, and crowds out project work. Chapter 5 argues each toil instance should either be automated or eliminated at the source.

questions.question: In the four golden signals framework, 'latency' specifically refers to:
options: The time to service a request, distinguishing successful request duration from failed or slow-error paths
Only network round-trip time between datacenters
CPU scheduling delay on the host regardless of request outcome
Time until the next deployment completes
questions.explanation: Chapter 6 defines latency as time to serve a request, emphasizing that failed requests can return quickly (e.g., 500 errors) and must be tracked separately from successful-path latency to avoid misleading dashboards.

questions.question: A service shows low CPU utilization but rising request queue depth and growing p99 latency. Which golden signal best explains the user-visible degradation?
options: Saturation: a resource limit (threads, connections, or I/O) is exhausted even though CPU appears idle
Traffic: request volume has necessarily dropped
Errors: all failures are client-side 404 responses
Latency is irrelevant when CPU is below 50%
questions.explanation: Saturation captures how 'full' a service is — thread pools, connection limits, disk I/O. Chapter 6 warns that CPU alone misleads; queue buildup with tail latency growth is classic saturation even at moderate CPU.

questions.question: Chapter 6 recommends alerting on symptoms rather than causes. Which alert rule best follows this principle?
options: Page when user-visible error rate exceeds SLO budget burn threshold for 15 minutes
Page when any single host's disk reaches 70% capacity
Page when a developer merges code to the main branch
Page when log volume increases 10% week-over-week
questions.explanation: Symptom-based alerting ties pages to user impact — error rate threatening SLOs — rather than low-level causes that may not affect users. Cause-based alerts belong in tickets or dashboards, not wake-up pages.

questions.question: During a gradual traffic shift from an old to a new API version, error rate stays flat but latency SLI degrades. Which golden-signal analysis is most appropriate first?
options: Compare traffic distribution and per-version latency distributions to detect a canary or routing regression
Assume errors will appear later and wait 24 hours before investigating
Disable all latency monitoring because error rate is stable
Increase traffic to the new version to average out latency spikes
questions.explanation: Traffic and latency must be examined together during rollouts. Chapter 6's golden signals are interdependent: a version receiving disproportionate load or exhibiting tail latency can breach SLOs before error rates move.

questions.question: In the SRE automation hierarchy, which level represents the highest maturity?
options: Autonomous systems that require no human intervention for routine operation and self-heal within defined bounds
Manual runbooks executed by on-call engineers for every change
Externally maintained tools that cannot be modified by the service team
Automation that runs once per quarter during maintenance windows only
questions.explanation: Chapter 7 describes automation maturity progressing from manual operation through externally owned tools, internally maintained automation, to autonomous systems. The goal is reducing operational toil while preserving human oversight for exceptional cases.

questions.question: A team relies on a central platform team's automation that breaks whenever the service changes its deployment model. Per chapter 7, the reliability gap is:
options: Dependence on externally maintained automation without internal ownership — the team should adopt or build internally maintained automation aligned to their service lifecycle
Automation is inherently unreliable and should be abandoned for manual operation
The platform team should be dissolved because shared tools never work
External automation is always superior to service-owned automation
questions.explanation: Chapter 7 ranks internally maintained automation above external tools because service teams can adapt automation to their changing needs. Relying on brittle external dependencies creates toil when the tool and service diverge.

questions.question: When evaluating whether to automate a rare but complex manual procedure, chapter 7 suggests the decision should weigh:
options: Frequency, error rate of manual execution, cost to automate, and cost of automation maintenance over the service lifetime
Only whether the procedure has ever caused an outage
Whether automation eliminates the need for on-call entirely
Whether the procedure can be completed in under five minutes manually
questions.explanation: Not every rare task merits automation. Chapter 7 advocates weighing execution frequency, human error risk, and long-term maintenance cost — automation itself can become a reliability burden if over-applied to edge cases.

questions.question: Hermetic build systems in release engineering primarily ensure:
options: Builds depend only on declared inputs, producing reproducible artifacts unaffected by ambient machine state
Builds run faster by skipping all dependency pinning
Only one engineer can trigger a production release per quarter
Source code is never stored in version control
questions.explanation: Chapter 8 emphasizes hermetic, reproducible builds as the foundation of reliable release engineering. Declared inputs eliminate 'works on my machine' drift and enable verifiable, auditable artifacts.

questions.question: A canary deployment shows elevated error rate on 5% of traffic while the stable version remains healthy. The release-engineering-aligned response is:
options: Automatically halt rollout and roll back the canary, preserving majority traffic on the known-good version while investigating
Continue rollout to 100% because 95% of traffic is fine
Remove canarying and deploy directly to all instances to finish faster
Disable monitoring during rollout to avoid false alarms
questions.explanation: Chapter 8 describes progressive rollout with automated rollback on regression signals. Canary analysis limits blast radius; continuing a bad rollout consumes error budget across the full fleet.

questions.question: Why does chapter 8 advocate separating release artifacts from configuration?
options: The same binary can be promoted across environments with environment-specific config, reducing rebuild risk and enabling faster rollback to a known artifact
Configuration should be compiled into binaries to prevent tampering
Artifacts and configuration must always be deployed in a single atomic blob with no separation
Separation increases the number of required manual steps per release
questions.explanation: Decoupling immutable artifacts from mutable configuration is core release engineering: promote tested binaries, apply config per environment, and roll back by reverting config or redeploying a prior artifact without rebuilding.

questions.question: Chapter 9 states that simplicity is a prerequisite for reliability because:
options: Complex systems have more interaction surfaces where failures emerge unpredictably and are harder to debug under pressure
Simple systems never require monitoring or on-call
Complexity always reduces operational cost per request
Simplicity means using the fewest lines of code regardless of clarity
questions.explanation: Chapter 9 argues complexity is the enemy of reliability: emergent failures, difficult reasoning under incident stress, and higher maintenance burden. Simplicity is intentional design restraint, not minimalism for its own sake.

questions.question: A team adds a third overlapping configuration system because the existing two are 'too simple' for edge cases. Chapter 9 would most likely warn that:
options: Accrued complexity increases long-term operational burden and incident confusion more than the edge cases justify
Multiple config systems always improve reliability through redundancy
Edge cases should drive unbounded system growth without consolidation plans
Simplicity applies only to user-facing UI, not backend infrastructure
questions.explanation: Chapter 9's simplicity principle opposes unnecessary accretion. Each new subsystem adds failure modes, cognitive load, and on-call surface area. Consolidation and deliberate abstraction beat parallel overlapping systems.

questions.question: When prioritizing simplicity work alongside feature requests, chapter 9 aligns with which approach?
options: Treat simplification as reliability engineering with measurable reduction in operational toil and incident frequency, not as optional cleanup
Defer all simplification until the service reaches 10x traffic growth
Simplicity work requires no metrics because it is purely aesthetic
Add complexity first and simplify only after a major outage occurs
questions.explanation: Chapter 9 frames simplicity as operational necessity, not vanity refactoring. Measuring toil reduction, faster incident resolution, and fewer moving parts justifies simplification alongside feature work — connecting to chapters 5 and 4's error-budget framing.
